extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. State vars: opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks (int[0,6]); initial opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0. Property G(opted_out implies not sent): in every reachable state where opted_out is true, sent must be false.

Scanning transitions for any that set sent:=true while opted_out is true: t_send_allowed sets sent:=true only when not opted_out (safe); t_send_blocked sets sent:=false when opted_out (safe); t_tick sets sent:=false (safe); t_optout sets opted_out:=true with sent:=false (safe). The outlier is t_resume_glitch (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=6: sent:=true) — it sets sent:=true precisely while opted_out is true, directly contradicting the property.

Reachability: ticks increments only via tick (capped at 6), so six tick events reach ticks=6. optout sets opted_out:=true (and sent:=false) while leaving ticks unchanged. With opted_out=true and ticks=6, a send_attempt enables t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out and ticks>=6 holds), setting sent:=true.

Counterexample (8 events, within the 10-event bound): tick x6 drives ticks 0->6; optout sets opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=6; send_attempt fires t_resume_glitch -> sent=true. Resulting reachable state opted_out=true AND sent=true violates G(opted_out implies not sent). Even though t_send_blocked also matches on that send_attempt, t_resume_glitch is an enabled, more-specific transition whose firing produces the violating state, which suffices to demonstrate that some execution violates the property. The rule is named resume_glitch — a deliberately injected defect. Verdict: violated.
